name: Aldol Condensation
summary: Two molecules of an aldehyde/ketone with alpha-H condense: enolate attacks a second carbonyl to give a beta-hydroxy carbonyl, which dehydrates on heating to an alpha,beta-unsaturated carbonyl.
memory_trick: Needs alpha-hydrogen on at least one partner; 'Aldol' = ALDehyde + alcOHol (the intermediate).

name: Cannizzaro Reaction
summary: Non-enolisable aldehydes (no alpha-H, e.g. HCHO, C6H5CHO) undergo self oxidation-reduction: one molecule is oxidised to carboxylate, the other reduced to alcohol.
memory_trick: No alpha-H = Cannizzaro. Crossed Cannizzaro with HCHO always sacrifices HCHO to give the other alcohol.

name: Sandmeyer Reaction
summary: Aryl diazonium salt is converted to aryl halide/nitrile via a Cu(I)-catalysed radical mechanism, replacing -N2+ with -Cl, -Br or -CN.
memory_trick: Sandmeyer = Cu salts. Gattermann variant uses Cu powder + HX instead of cuprous halide.

name: Gattermann Reaction
summary: Similar outcome to Sandmeyer (ArN2+ -> ArX) but uses copper powder with the corresponding acid instead of a cuprous salt.
memory_trick: Think 'Gattermann = cu powder', cheaper alternative to Sandmeyer's cuprous salts.

name: Reimer-Tiemann Reaction
summary: Phenol reacts with chloroform under strongly basic conditions via a dichlorocarbene intermediate, introducing a -CHO group ortho to -OH (salicylaldehyde formation).
memory_trick: 'Reimer-Tiemann' -> ring gets a -CHO ortho to OH; carbene (:CCl2) is the key electrophile.

name: Kolbe's Reaction
reagents_conditions: NaOH then CO2 (high P, T), then H3O+
summary: Sodium phenoxide reacts with CO2 to install -COOH ortho to the -OH group (electrophilic aromatic substitution by CO2), giving salicylic acid on acidification.
memory_trick: Kolbe gives -COOH (acid), Reimer-Tiemann gives -CHO (aldehyde) - both go ortho to OH.

name: Williamson Ether Synthesis
summary: An alkoxide ion displaces a halide from a (preferably primary) alkyl halide in an SN2 step to form an unsymmetrical or symmetrical ether.
memory_trick: Always use the bulkier group as the alkoxide and the LESS hindered halide as the electrophile (SN2 needs primary substrate).

name: HVZ Reaction (Hell-Volhard-Zelinsky)
summary: Carboxylic acids with an alpha-hydrogen are selectively halogenated at the alpha-carbon via the acid's enol-like bromide intermediate, giving alpha-halo acids.
memory_trick: HVZ only works on acids (needs alpha-H); red P generates PX3 in situ which activates the -COOH.

name: Etard Reaction
reagents_conditions: CrO2Cl2 (chromyl chloride) in CS2, then hydrolysis
summary: Toluene (or a methylbenzene) is oxidised in a controlled way to benzaldehyde, stopping short of the carboxylic acid, via a chromium complex intermediate.
memory_trick: Etard = controlled oxidation that stops at -CHO (unlike KMnO4 which over-oxidises toluene to -COOH).

name: Rosenmund Reduction
reagents_conditions: H2 / Pd-BaSO4 (poisoned catalyst, + S or quinoline)
summary: Acyl chlorides are selectively reduced to aldehydes; the catalyst is 'poisoned' with BaSO4/quinoline so reduction stops at the aldehyde stage instead of going to the alcohol.
memory_trick: Poisoned Pd = stop at aldehyde. Without poisoning, H2/Pd would over-reduce to the alcohol.

name: Clemmensen Reduction
summary: Reduces the C=O of an aldehyde or ketone all the way to CH2 (deoxygenation) under strongly acidic conditions - useful when the substrate is acid-stable.
memory_trick: Acidic deoxygenation = Clemmensen. Basic deoxygenation (for acid-sensitive substrates) = Wolff-Kishner.

name: Wolff-Kishner Reduction
summary: Converts a carbonyl to CH2 via a hydrazone intermediate that loses N2 under strongly basic, high-temperature conditions - the base-tolerant counterpart to Clemmensen.
memory_trick: Basic deoxygenation = Wolff-Kishner (use when substrate can't survive strong acid).

name: Friedel-Crafts Alkylation
summary: A Lewis-acid-generated carbocation alkylates an aromatic ring; prone to carbocation rearrangement and polyalkylation, and fails on strongly deactivated rings (e.g. nitrobenzene).
memory_trick: F-C alkylation fails on aniline/nitrobenzene (deactivated/complexed rings); watch for carbocation rearrangement with secondary/tertiary R-X.

name: Friedel-Crafts Acylation
summary: An acylium ion (no rearrangement risk) acylates the ring to give an aryl ketone; self-limiting because the product ketone deactivates the ring, avoiding polysubstitution.
memory_trick: Acylation > alkylation in synthesis because acylium ions don't rearrange and the reaction self-terminates (no polysubstitution).

name: Wurtz Reaction
summary: Two alkyl halide molecules couple via a free-radical/organosodium pathway to form a longer symmetrical alkane, releasing NaX.
memory_trick: Only makes symmetrical alkanes with an EVEN number of carbons total (adding two identical R groups).

name: Wurtz-Fittig Reaction
reagents_conditions: Aryl halide + alkyl halide + Na (dry ether)
summary: A mixed coupling of an aryl halide with an alkyl halide using sodium, forming an alkylbenzene (aryl-alkyl C-C bond).
example: C6H5Br + CH3Br --Na, dry ether--> C6H5CH3 + 2 NaBr
memory_trick: Aryl + alkyl halide together = Wurtz-Fittig (vs plain Wurtz which is alkyl + alkyl only).

name: Markovnikov's Rule Addition
summary: H adds to the carbon of the double bond that already has more hydrogens, so X ends up on the more substituted carbon, via the more stable carbocation intermediate.
memory_trick: 'The rich get richer' - H goes to the carbon with more H's already.

name: Anti-Markovnikov / Kharasch (Peroxide) Effect
summary: Only with HBr (not HCl/HI) and peroxides, a free-radical chain mechanism reverses the regiochemistry so Br ends up on the less substituted carbon.
memory_trick: Peroxide effect works ONLY with HBr - HCl and HI bond energies don't support the radical chain.

name: Diazotisation
summary: A primary aromatic amine reacts with nitrous acid (generated in situ) at low temperature to form a stable aryl diazonium salt - a versatile synthetic handle for many substitutions.
memory_trick: Must be kept below 5 C - diazonium salts decompose above ~10 C, releasing N2.

name: Carbylamine (Isocyanide) Reaction
summary: A classic qualitative test: primary amines (aliphatic or aromatic) form a foul-smelling isocyanide with chloroform and alcoholic KOH; secondary/tertiary amines don't react.
memory_trick: Positive carbylamine test = confirms a 1° amine (distinguishes it from 2°/3° amines).

name: Hofmann Bromamide Degradation
summary: A primary amide is converted to a primary amine with one fewer carbon, via an isocyanate intermediate formed by a nitrene-like rearrangement.
memory_trick: Hofmann degradation: amide -> amine, losing one carbon (as CO2/carbonate) - useful for 'stepping down' a carbon chain.

name: Gabriel Phthalimide Synthesis
summary: Potassium phthalimide is alkylated by a primary alkyl halide, then hydrolysed to release a pure primary amine free of 2°/3° amine contamination.
memory_trick: Gabriel synthesis gives ONLY 1° amines cleanly - can't be used for aromatic amines (ArX doesn't undergo SN2).

name: Aldol / Perkin Reaction (Perkin Condensation)
reagents_conditions: Aromatic aldehyde + acid anhydride + sodium salt of the acid (base)
summary: An aromatic aldehyde condenses with an acid anhydride (acting as the enolisable partner) under basic catalysis to give an alpha,beta-unsaturated aromatic acid.
memory_trick: Perkin uses an anhydride (not just an aldehyde/ketone pair) as the active-methylene component.

name: Schotten-Baumann Reaction
reagents_conditions: Amine or alcohol + acid chloride, aqueous NaOH
summary: An amine (or alcohol) is acylated by an acid chloride in the presence of aqueous base, which mops up the HCl byproduct and drives the reaction to the amide/ester.
memory_trick: The base's job is simply to neutralise HCl as it forms, pushing the acylation to completion.

name: Fittig Reaction
summary: Two molecules of an aryl halide couple with sodium to form a biaryl (diphenyl-type) compound, the aromatic analogue of the Wurtz reaction.
memory_trick: Fittig = aryl + aryl coupling (biphenyl formation); Wurtz-Fittig = aryl + alkyl instead.

name: Balz-Schiemann Reaction
summary: An aryl diazonium tetrafluoroborate salt decomposes on heating to give an aryl fluoride - the standard way to introduce fluorine onto a benzene ring (Sandmeyer doesn't work for F).
memory_trick: Use Balz-Schiemann specifically for Ar-F; Sandmeyer/Gattermann cover Cl, Br, CN but not F.

name: Lucas Test
summary: A qualitative test that distinguishes 1°, 2°, 3° alcohols by the rate at which they turn cloudy (form the alkyl chloride) - 3° reacts instantly, 2° in minutes, 1° barely at room temperature.
memory_trick: Instant cloudiness = 3° alcohol; needs heating and stays clear a while = 1° alcohol.
